description: Classroom discourse talk moves from math lessons

classes.name: Keeping Everyone Together
classes.description: Teacher manages turns, attention, or involvement of the whole class. Includes yes/no checks, reminders, call-and-response, prompting students to be active listeners.
examples: Does everyone see that?
Are you listening?
All eyes on me
Whole class, what is...?
Put your hands up if you agree

classes.name: Getting Students to Relate
classes.description: Teacher asks a student to comment on, agree/disagree with, or build on another student's idea. Must go beyond simple repetition of the peer statement.
examples: Do you agree with what she said?
Can you build on his idea?
What do you think about what Jamie said?
Does anyone have a different thought?
How is your idea similar to Juan's?

classes.name: Restating
classes.description: Teacher repeats a student's utterance word-for-word, without adding or changing wording.
examples: So you're saying the area is 24
You think we need to divide by 2
So the answer is 12

classes.name: Pressing for Accuracy
classes.description: Teacher requests a short, precise answer, walks through procedures step-by-step, checks definitions or names of mathematical objects.
examples: What is the exact term for that shape?
Walk us through the first step
What's the precise definition?
How do you call that property?
What number specifically?

classes.name: Revoicing
classes.description: Teacher repeats a student's idea while adding to it, rephrasing, correcting, or translating into another language or notation.
examples: So you're saying that x represents the length, which is the same as the base
She noticed the pattern, which means she saw a rule
His answer was close, but the exact term is 'perimeter'

classes.name: Pressing for Reasoning
classes.description: Teacher asks for explanations, 'why' questions, connections between ideas, or comparisons of representations.
examples: Why does that work?
How do you know?
What's the reason?
Can you explain your thinking?
How is this representation different from that one?

classes.name: Relating to Another Student
classes.description: Student builds on, repeats, questions, or responds directly to a peer's contribution.
examples: I agree with Maria because...
What Jamie said makes me think...
I see it differently - let me explain
Following up on Sam's idea...

classes.name: Asking for More Information
classes.description: Student expresses confusion, asks clarifying questions, or requests elaboration.
examples: I don't understand, can you explain again?
What do you mean by 'that'?
Could you give an example?
I'm confused about the second step
Wait, why did you do that?

classes.name: Making a Claim
classes.description: Student states an answer, defines a quantity, or asserts a fact without explaining the reasoning.
examples: The answer is 42
That's a parallelogram
The area is 24 square units
That's not correct
It goes up by 3 each time

classes.name: Providing Evidence or Reasoning
classes.description: Student justifies a claim, describes multi-step procedures, notices patterns, or compares representations.
examples: It must be 12 because 3 times 4 equals 12
I know it's a rectangle because it has 4 right angles
The pattern adds 5 each step so the next is 20
I used the formula by multiplying length times width
